content:
# Exodus Chapter 25 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~46v-47r (PDF pp. 91-93); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 67-68 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_025_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_025_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_025_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_025_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

> **MANUSCRIPT NOTE -- THE TERUMAH INSTRUCTIONS:** The Leningrad Codex presents Exodus 25 in standard prose column format with full Masoretic vocalization and cantillation. This chapter opens the massive tabernacle instruction block (chs. 25-31), delivered during Moses' forty days on the mountain (24:18). The Hebrew term *terumah* ("contribution, heave-offering") gives the parashah its name. The chapter moves from the voluntary collection of materials (vv. 1-9) to three sacred furnishings: the Ark of the Covenant (vv. 10-22), the Table of the Bread of the Presence (vv. 23-30), and the Golden Lampstand (vv. 31-40). The keyword *tavnit* ("pattern, model") in vv. 9 and 40 frames the entire section as an inclusio, establishing that the earthly tabernacle replicates a heavenly archetype. The Vaticanus manuscript (pp. 67-68) presents the LXX in continuous uncial script; the versification aligns with the MT throughout, though the LXX frequently renders Hebrew technical terms with Greek architectural and liturgical equivalents.

---

## The Offering for the Tabernacle (vv. 1-9)

**25:1** And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying: [fn1]

**25:2** "Speak to the Sons of Israel and let them take for me a contribution; from every man whose heart moves him you shall take my contribution. [fn2]

**25:3** And this is the contribution which you shall take from them: gold and silver and bronze, [fn3]

**25:4** and blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine linen and goat hair, [fn4]

**25:5** and ram skins dyed red and takhash skins and acacia wood, [fn5]

**25:6** oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense, [fn6]

**25:7** onyx stones and stones for setting for the ephod and for the breastpiece. [fn7]

**25:8** And let them make for me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in their midst. [fn8]

**25:9** According to all that I am showing you -- the pattern of the tabernacle and the pattern of all its vessels -- so you shall make it." [fn9]

---

## The Ark of the Testimony (vv. 10-22)

**25:10** "And they shall make an ark of acacia wood: two cubits and a half its length, and a cubit and a half its width, and a cubit and a half its height. [fn10]

**25:11** And you shall overlay it with pure gold; from inside and from outside you shall overlay it, and you shall make upon it a molding of gold all around. [fn11]

**25:12** And you shall cast for it four rings of gold and put them upon its four feet, and two rings upon its one side and two rings upon its second side. [fn12]

**25:13** And you shall make poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold. [fn13]

**25:14** And you shall bring the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark, to carry the ark by them. [fn14]

**25:15** In the rings of the ark the poles shall be; they shall not be removed from it. [fn15]

**25:16** And you shall put into the ark the testimony which I will give to you. [fn16]

**25:17** And you shall make a mercy seat of pure gold: two cubits and a half its length and a cubit and a half its width. [fn17]

**25:18** And you shall make two cherubim of gold; of hammered work you shall make them, from the two ends of the mercy seat. [fn18]

**25:19** And make one cherub from this end and one cherub from that end; from the mercy seat you shall make the cherubim on its two ends. [fn19]

**25:20** And the cherubim shall be spreading their wings upward, covering with their wings over the mercy seat, and their faces each toward his brother; toward the mercy seat shall be the faces of the cherubim. [fn20]

**25:21** And you shall put the mercy seat on top of the ark from above, and into the ark you shall put the testimony which I will give to you. [fn21]

**25:22** And I will meet with you there, and I will speak with you from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim which are upon the ark of the testimony, all that I will command you for the Sons of Israel." [fn22]

---

## The Table of the Bread of the Presence (vv. 23-30)

**25:23** "And you shall make a table of acacia wood: two cubits its length, and a cubit its width, and a cubit and a half its height. [fn23]

**25:24** And you shall overlay it with pure gold and make for it a molding of gold all around. [fn24]

**25:25** And you shall make for it a border a handbreadth wide all around, and you shall make a molding of gold for its border all around. [fn25]

**25:26** And you shall make for it four rings of gold and put the rings upon the four corners which are at its four legs. [fn26]

**25:27** Close to the border the rings shall be, as holders for the poles to carry the table. [fn27]

**25:28** And you shall make the poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold, and the table shall be carried with them. [fn28]

**25:29** And you shall make its dishes and its ladles and its jars and its bowls with which it will be poured out; of pure gold you shall make them. [fn29]

**25:30** And you shall set upon the table the bread of the Presence before me continually." [fn30]

---

## The Golden Lampstand (vv. 31-40)

**25:31** "And you shall make a lampstand of pure gold; of hammered work the lampstand shall be made. Its base and its shaft, its cups, its knobs, and its blossoms shall be from it -- of one piece with it. [fn31]

**25:32** And six branches going out from its sides: three branches of the lampstand from its one side and three branches of the lampstand from its second side. [fn32]

**25:33** Three cups shaped like almond blossoms on the one branch -- a knob and a blossom; and three cups shaped like almond blossoms on the other branch -- a knob and a blossom; so for the six branches going out from the lampstand. [fn33]

**25:34** And on the lampstand four cups shaped like almond blossoms -- its knobs and its blossoms. [fn34]

**25:35** And a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it -- for the six branches going out from the lampstand. [fn35]

**25:36** Their knobs and their branches shall be from it; all of it one hammered work of pure gold. [fn36]

**25:37** And you shall make its lamps seven, and he shall set up its lamps and it shall give light upon the space before it. [fn37]

**25:38** And its tongs and its firepans of pure gold. [fn38]

**25:39** A talent of pure gold he shall make it, with all these vessels. [fn39]

**25:40** And see and make according to their pattern which you are being shown on the mountain." [fn40]

---

## Translator's Notes

### Structural Note

Exodus 25 inaugurates the great tabernacle instruction section (chs. 25-31), the longest continuous divine speech in the Torah, delivered during Moses' forty days within the cloud on Sinai (24:18). The chapter divides into four sections: (1) the call for offerings and the statement of purpose (vv. 1-9), (2) the ark of the testimony (vv. 10-22), (3) the table of the bread of the Presence (vv. 23-30), and (4) the golden lampstand (vv. 31-40). The literary order begins with the ark -- the innermost and holiest furnishing -- and works outward: God starts from his own throne and moves toward the people. The keyword *tavnit* ("pattern") in vv. 9 and 40 forms an inclusio, establishing that the earthly tabernacle replicates a heavenly archetype shown to Moses on the mountain.

### Key Decisions

**"Contribution" for *terumah* (vv. 2-3):** EMET renders "contribution," LOGOS renders "offering," PNEUMA renders "offering." The consensus follows EMET -- "contribution" for *terumah* (from *rum*, "to lift up, to raise") preserves the distinction from other offering terms (*qorban*, *zevakh*, *minkhah*) and captures the sense of something voluntarily "raised up" and set apart. "Offering" is too generic and risks confusion with sacrificial offerings placed on the altar.

**"Moves him" for *yiddevennu* (v. 2):** All three translators agree on the sense of voluntary internal impulse. EMET renders "moves him," LOGOS renders "moves him," PNEUMA renders "moves them to give." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "moves him" for *yiddevennu* (qal imperfect of *nadav*, "to impel, move voluntarily" + 3ms suffix) preserves the singular, matching the Hebrew *kol-ish* ("every man") and the active role of the heart as grammatical subject.

**"Takhash skins" for *orot tekhashim* (v. 5):** EMET leaves *takhash* untranslated. LOGOS renders "tahash skins." PNEUMA renders "fine leather." The consensus follows EMET -- the identity of the *takhash* is genuinely uncertain (proposals include dugong, dolphin, seal, or a type of fine leather), and the term is best left transliterated as "takhash" rather than guessed at. PNEUMA's "fine leather" imports an interpretation not in the text.

**"Molding" for *zer* (vv. 11, 24, 25):** EMET renders "molding," LOGOS renders "rim," PNEUMA renders "molding." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "molding" for *zer* ("border, crown-molding") captures the decorative rim or crown-like border around the top edge. LOGOS's "rim" is too generic, losing the sense of ornamental craftsmanship.

**"Mercy seat" for *kapporet* (vv. 17-22):** All three translators agree on "mercy seat." This rendering, following Tyndale's tradition (via Luther's *Gnadenstuhl*), preserves the connection to *kaphar* ("to cover, atone, ransom") while indicating the surface's function as both cover and place of propitiation. The LXX *hilasterion* ("place of propitiation") supports this theological rendering.

**"Knobs" for *kaftorim* (vv. 31-36):** EMET renders "knobs," LOGOS renders "buds," PNEUMA renders "buds." The consensus follows EMET -- "knobs" for *kaftorim* preserves the architectural/ornamental sense of the Hebrew term, which denotes bulbous ornamental protrusions on the lampstand. "Buds" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) imports a botanical interpretation that, while contextually plausible given the almond-blossom motif, narrows the semantic range of the Hebrew.

**"Firepans" for *makhtotot* (v. 38):** EMET renders "firepans," LOGOS renders "fire pans," PNEUMA renders "trays." The consensus follows EMET -- "firepans" for *makhtotot* (from *khatah*, "to snatch up coals") preserves the etymological connection to fire/coals. PNEUMA's "trays" is too generic.

**"Border" for *misgeret* (v. 25):** EMET renders "border," LOGOS renders "frame," PNEUMA renders "rim." The consensus follows EMET -- "border" for *misgeret* (from *sagar*, "to close, shut") denotes an enclosing frame or raised edge around the table. LOGOS's "frame" implies a structural element, and PNEUMA's "rim" is too thin in connotation.

### Vaticanus (LXX) Divergences

The Vaticanus text of Exodus 25 (pp. 67-68) preserves several notable divergences from the MT: (1) In v. 2, the LXX renders *terumah* as *aparchas* ("first-fruits, first-portions"), importing an agricultural-offering concept absent from the Hebrew. (2) In v. 5, the LXX translates *tekhashim* as *huakinthina* ("hyacinth-colored"), interpreting the unknown animal as a color designation rather than preserving the ambiguity. (3) In v. 8, the LXX reads *ophthosomai* ("I will be seen/appear") for *veshakhanti* ("I will dwell"), replacing the theology of indwelling with theophanic visibility -- a significant theological shift that may reflect a more transcendent theology of divine presence. (4) In v. 9, the LXX adds *en to orei* ("on the mountain"), making explicit what the MT leaves implicit. The LXX renders *tavnit* as *paradeigma* ("pattern, paradigm"), while in v. 40 it uses *tupos* ("type, pattern") -- the term quoted in Hebrews 8:5. (5) In v. 10, the LXX adds *marturiou* ("of testimony") to *kibotos* ("ark"), importing the full title prematurely. (6) In v. 22, the LXX reads *gnosthesomai* ("I will make myself known") for *veno'adti* ("I will meet by appointment"), shifting from spatial encounter to cognitive revelation. (7) Throughout, the LXX translates *shittim* ("acacia") as *ashepta* ("incorruptible wood"), describing the quality rather than the species, and renders *miqshah* ("hammered work") as *toreute* ("wrought, turned on a lathe"), a difference in craft terminology.

---

> **[fn1] v.1 -- *Va-yedabber YHWH el-Mosheh le'mor*: "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "And YHWH spoke to Moses, saying." LOGOS renders "And YHWH spoke to Moses saying." PNEUMA renders "YHWH spoke to Moses." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "saying" for *le'mor* (lamed + infinitive construct of *amar*) is preserved as it marks the transition from narrative frame to direct speech. PNEUMA omits *le'mor* entirely, losing the formal speech-introduction formula that opens each new divine instruction block.

> **[fn2] v.2 -- *Dabber el-benei Yisra'el veyiqchu-li terumah me'et kol-ish asher yiddevennu libbo tiqchu et-terumati*: "Speak to the Sons of Israel and let them take for me a contribution; from every man whose heart moves him you shall take my contribution"** -- EMET renders "Speak to the Sons of Israel and let them take for me a contribution; from every man whose heart moves him, you shall take my contribution." LOGOS renders "Speak to the Sons of Israel and let them take for Me an offering. From every man whose heart moves him you shall take My offering." PNEUMA renders "Tell the Sons of Israel to bring me an offering. You are to receive my offering from everyone whose heart moves them to give." The consensus follows EMET for *terumah* as "contribution" (see Key Decisions above). PNEUMA's "tell" for *dabber* loses the formal register of the piel imperative; "speak" is preferred. PNEUMA's "bring" for *veyiqchu* reverses the verb's direction -- the Hebrew says "take" (*laqakh*), not "bring" (*bo* hiphil). PNEUMA's addition of "to give" is not in the Hebrew. The shift from plural "let them take" to singular "every man whose heart" is preserved, reflecting the communal act arising from individual willing.

> **[fn3] v.3 -- *Vezot hatterumah asher tiqchu me'ittam zahav vakhesef unechoshet*: "And this is the contribution which you shall take from them: gold and silver and bronze"** -- All three translators agree. The three metals are listed in descending value, corresponding to zones of holiness in the tabernacle: gold for the innermost sanctuary, silver for structural elements, bronze for the outer court.

> **[fn4] v.4 -- *Utekheleth ve'argaman vetola'at shani veshesh ve'izzim*: "And blue and purple and scarlet yarn and fine linen and goat hair"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *ve'izzim*, EMET renders "goat hair," LOGOS renders "goats' hair," PNEUMA renders "goat hair." The consensus follows EMET/PNEUMA -- "goat hair" without the possessive, since the Hebrew *izzim* ("goats") is shorthand for the material. The Hebrew *tola'at shani* (literally "worm of scarlet") refers to crimson dye from the kermes insect; all three translators render this as "scarlet yarn," which captures the finished product.

> **[fn5] v.5 -- *Ve'orot eilim me'oddamim ve'orot tekhashim va'atsei shittim*: "And ram skins dyed red and takhash skins and acacia wood"** -- EMET renders "skins of rams made red and skins of takhash and acacia wood." LOGOS renders "rams' skins dyed red, and tahash skins, and acacia wood." PNEUMA renders "ram skins dyed red, fine leather, and acacia wood." The consensus renders "ram skins dyed red and takhash skins" -- simplifying EMET's word order while preserving "takhash" untranslated (see Key Decisions). PNEUMA's "fine leather" for *orot tekhashim* is rejected as interpretive.

> **[fn6] v.6 -- *Shemen lamma'or besamim leshemen hammishchah veliqqtoret hassammim*: "Oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "oil for the light, spices for the oil of anointing and for the incense of fragrances." LOGOS renders "oil for the light, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense." PNEUMA renders "oil for the lamps, spices for the anointing oil and for the fragrant incense." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "the anointing oil" for *shemen hammishchah* and "the fragrant incense" for *qetoret hassammim* are natural English constructions that preserve the Hebrew genitives. EMET's "oil of anointing" and "incense of fragrances" are hyper-literal. PNEUMA's "lamps" (plural) for *lamma'or* (singular, "for the light/luminary") is inaccurate at this point -- the lampstand has not yet been described.

> **[fn7] v.7 -- *Avnei-shoham ve'avnei millu'im la'efod velachoshen*: "Onyx stones and stones for setting for the ephod and for the breastpiece"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "onyx stones and stones of setting." LOGOS renders "onyx stones and setting stones." The consensus renders "onyx stones and stones for setting" -- the *millu'im* ("settings, fillings," from *male*, "to fill") are stones set into mountings, and "for setting" clarifies the function. The identification of *shoham* as "onyx" follows the traditional rendering, though the exact stone is uncertain. Both *efod* and *choshen* are left in their standard English forms ("ephod," "breastpiece").

> **[fn8] v.8 -- *Ve'asu li miqdash veshakhanti betokham*: "And let them make for me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in their midst"** -- All three translators agree on this theologically momentous verse. EMET renders "And let them make for me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in their midst." LOGOS renders "And let them make for Me a sanctuary, and I will dwell in their midst." PNEUMA renders "And let them build me a sanctuary, so that I may dwell among them." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS. PNEUMA's "build" for *ve'asu* (from *asah*, "to make, do") loses the broader sense of the verb -- the tabernacle is "made," not merely "built," encompassing all the textile, metalwork, and woodwork involved. PNEUMA's "so that I may dwell" imports a purpose-clause construction not present in the Hebrew, which uses a simple *vav*-consecutive ("and I will dwell"). The preposition *betokham* ("in their midst") is critically "in their midst," not "in its midst" -- YHWH's stated purpose is to dwell among persons, not merely to inhabit a structure. The verb *shakan* ("to dwell, tabernacle") is the root of *mishkan* ("tabernacle") and the later concept of *shekhinah*.

> **[fn9] v.9 -- *Kekhol asher ani mar'eh otekha et tavnit hammishkan ve'et tavnit kol-kelav vekhen ta'asu*: "According to all that I am showing you -- the pattern of the tabernacle and the pattern of all its vessels -- so you shall make it"** -- EMET renders "According to all that I am showing you, the pattern of the tabernacle and the pattern of all its vessels, and thus you shall make." LOGOS renders "According to all that I am showing you -- the pattern of the Tabernacle and the pattern of all its vessels -- so you shall make it." PNEUMA renders "You must make it -- the dwelling place and all its furnishings -- exactly according to the pattern I am about to show you." The consensus follows LOGOS for the em-dash syntax, which effectively sets off the explanatory phrase. PNEUMA restructures the verse entirely, placing "you must make it" first and converting *tavnit* to a prepositional phrase at the end; this loses the Hebrew word order, which foregrounds "according to all that I am showing you." PNEUMA's "dwelling place" for *mishkan* loses the technical term "tabernacle." PNEUMA's "furnishings" for *kelav* ("vessels, implements") is too free. EMET's "and thus" for *vekhen* is slightly awkward; LOGOS's "so" is preferred. The term *tavnit* ("pattern," from *banah*, "to build") recurs at v. 40, forming the chapter's inclusio.

> **[fn10] v.10 -- *Ve'asu aron atsei shittim ammatayim vachetsi orkho ve'ammah vachetsi rokhbo ve'ammah vachetsi qomato*: "And they shall make an ark of acacia wood: two cubits and a half its length, and a cubit and a half its width, and a cubit and a half its height"** -- All three translators agree. The *aron* ("ark, chest, box") is the first object described -- the most sacred furnishing, which will stand in the innermost room. The dimensions (2.5 x 1.5 x 1.5 cubits, approximately 3.75 x 2.25 x 2.25 feet) are given in half-cubits. PNEUMA drops the conjunction "And" and renders "They are to make" -- the consensus preserves the *vav*-consecutive construction.

> **[fn11] v.11 -- *Vetsippita oto zahav tahor mibbayit umichutz tetsappennu ve'asita alav zer zahav saviv*: "And you shall overlay it with pure gold; from inside and from outside you shall overlay it, and you shall make upon it a molding of gold all around"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *zer*, see Key Decisions ("molding" preferred over LOGOS's "rim"). The double overlay -- inside and out -- means the acacia wood core is entirely sheathed in gold. The verb *tsafah* (piel, "to overlay, plate") is used for gold-plating throughout the tabernacle instructions.

> **[fn12] v.12 -- *Veyatsaqta lo arba tabb'ot zahav venatattha al arba pa'amotav ushtei tabba'ot al-tsal'o ha'echat ushtei tabba'ot al-tsal'o hashshenit*: "And you shall cast for it four rings of gold and put them upon its four feet, and two rings upon its one side and two rings upon its second side"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *pa'amotav*, EMET/LOGOS render "feet," PNEUMA renders "feet." For *tsal'o*, all render "side." The distribution of rings (two per side) provides the attachment points for the carrying poles.

> **[fn13] v.13 -- *Ve'asita baddei atsei shittim vetsippita otam zahav*: "And you shall make poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold"** -- All three translators agree. The *baddim* ("poles, staves") are the carrying poles inserted through the rings.

> **[fn14] v.14 -- *Veheve'ta et-habbaddim battabba'ot al tsal'ot ha'aron laset et-ha'aron bahem*: "And you shall bring the poles into the rings on the sides of the ark, to carry the ark by them"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "to carry the ark with them." LOGOS renders "to carry the ark by them." PNEUMA renders "so that the ark can be carried by them." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "by them" for *bahem* (instrumental use of *be-*). PNEUMA's passive construction loses the active sense.

> **[fn15] v.15 -- *Betabb'ot ha'aron yihyu habbaddim lo yasuru mimmennu*: "In the rings of the ark the poles shall be; they shall not be removed from it"** -- All three translators agree. The permanent placement of the poles is unique among the tabernacle furnishings -- the ark must always be ready to move, always portable, never settled.

> **[fn16] v.16 -- *Venatattha el-ha'aron et-ha'edut asher etten elekha*: "And you shall put into the ark the testimony which I will give to you"** -- All three translators agree. The *edut* ("testimony") refers to the stone tablets as the covenant witness-document.

> **[fn17] v.17 -- *Ve'asita kapporet zahav tahor ammatayim vachetsi orkah ve'ammah vachetsi rokhbah*: "And you shall make a mercy seat of pure gold: two cubits and a half its length and a cubit and a half its width"** -- All three translators agree on "mercy seat" for *kapporet* (see Key Decisions). The dimensions match the ark exactly, as the *kapporet* serves as its cover.

> **[fn18] v.18 -- *Ve'asita shnayim keruvim zahav miqshah ta'aseh otam mishshnei qetsot hakkapporet*: "And you shall make two cherubim of gold; of hammered work you shall make them, from the two ends of the mercy seat"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The *miqshah* ("hammered work") means the cherubim are hammered from a single piece of gold, not cast or assembled separately. PNEUMA renders "hammered gold" rather than "of hammered work" -- the consensus preserves the technical term as a manner-of-construction specification.

> **[fn19] v.19 -- *Va'aseh keruv echad miqqatseh mizzeh ukeruv-echad miqqatseh mizzeh min-hakkapporet ta'asu et-hakkeruvim al-shnei qetsotav*: "And make one cherub from this end and one cherub from that end; from the mercy seat you shall make the cherubim on its two ends"** -- EMET renders "from this end...from this end." LOGOS renders "from this end...from that end." PNEUMA renders "at one end...at the other end." The consensus follows LOGOS -- "from this end...from that end" captures the Hebrew *mizzeh...mizzeh* (literally "from this...from this," meaning "from one side...from the other side") more naturally than EMET's repetition, while PNEUMA's "at" loses the partitive *min* ("from") that indicates the cherubim are formed out of the mercy seat itself.

> **[fn20] v.20 -- *Vehayu hakkeruvim porsei khenafayim lema'lah sokhekhim bekhanfeihem al-hakkapporet ufeneihem ish el-achiv el-hakkapporet yihyu penei hakkeruvim*: "And the cherubim shall be spreading their wings upward, covering with their wings over the mercy seat, and their faces each toward his brother; toward the mercy seat shall be the faces of the cherubim"** -- EMET renders "spreading...covering." LOGOS renders "spreading...covering." PNEUMA renders "have their wings spread...sheltering." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- the Hebrew participles *porsei* and *sokhekhim* are rendered as progressive participles ("spreading...covering") preserving the ongoing action. PNEUMA's "sheltering" for *sokhekhim* (from *sakhakh*, "to cover, screen, protect") is acceptable but less precise. For *ish el-akhiv*, all three render "each toward his brother/the other" -- the consensus preserves the literal "each toward his brother."

> **[fn21] v.21 -- *Venatattha et-hakkapporet al-ha'aron milema'lah ve'el-ha'aron titten et-ha'edut asher etten elekha*: "And you shall put the mercy seat on top of the ark from above, and into the ark you shall put the testimony which I will give to you"** -- All three translators agree. PNEUMA renders "Set the mercy seat on top of the ark, and place inside the ark the testimony." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS in preserving "from above" for *milema'lah*, which PNEUMA omits.

> **[fn22] v.22 -- *Veno'adti lekha sham vedibbarthi ittekha me'al hakkapporet mibbein shnei hakkeruvim asher al-aron ha'edut et kol-asher atsavveh otekha el-benei Yisra'el*: "And I will meet with you there, and I will speak with you from above the mercy seat, from between the two cherubim which are upon the ark of the testimony, all that I will command you for the Sons of Israel"** -- All three translators agree on the substance of this theological climax. The verb *no'adti* (niphal of *ya'ad*, "to appoint a meeting") indicates the mercy seat is the appointed meeting-place between YHWH and Moses. The final preposition *el* before "the Sons of Israel" is rendered "for" (LOGOS) rather than EMET's "to" -- the commands are given to Moses *concerning* or *for* the Sons of Israel, not *to* them directly. PNEUMA renders "concerning" for *el*, which captures the sense but loses the preposition's form. The consensus follows LOGOS's "for."

> **[fn23] v.23 -- *Ve'asita shulchan atsei shittim ammatayim orkho ve'ammah rokhbo ve'ammah vachetsi qomato*: "And you shall make a table of acacia wood: two cubits its length, and a cubit its width, and a cubit and a half its height"** -- All three translators agree. The table dimensions (2 x 1 x 1.5 cubits) are smaller than the ark in length but equal in height.

> **[fn24] v.24 -- *Vetsippita oto zahav tahor ve'asita lo zer zahav saviv*: "And you shall overlay it with pure gold and make for it a molding of gold all around"** -- All three translators agree. See fn11 for *zer* as "molding."

> **[fn25] v.25 -- *Ve'asita lo misgeret tofach saviv ve'asita zer-zahav lemisgaretto saviv*: "And you shall make for it a border a handbreadth wide all around, and you shall make a molding of gold for its border all around"** -- EMET renders "border." LOGOS renders "frame." PNEUMA renders "rim." The consensus follows EMET -- "border" for *misgeret* (see Key Decisions). The *tefakh* ("handbreadth," approximately 3 inches / 7.5 cm) specifies the width of this enclosing edge.

> **[fn26] v.26 -- *Ve'asita lo arba tabb'ot zahav venatattha et-hattabba'ot al arba happi'ot asher le'arba raglav*: "And you shall make for it four rings of gold and put the rings upon the four corners which are at its four legs"** -- All three translators agree.

> **[fn27] v.27 -- *Le'ummat hammisgeret tihyeynah hattabb'ot lebattim labaddim laset et-hashulchan*: "Close to the border the rings shall be, as holders for the poles to carry the table"** -- All three translators agree in substance. For *lebattim*, EMET renders "as holders," LOGOS renders "as holders," PNEUMA renders "to hold the poles." The consensus follows EMET/LOGOS -- "as holders" preserves *batim* ("houses") as noun, indicating the rings serve as "housings" for the poles.

> **[fn28] v.28 -- *Ve'asita et-habbaddim atsei shittim vetsippita otam zahav venissa-bam et-hashulchan*: "And you shall make the poles of acacia wood and overlay them with gold, and the table shall be carried with them"** -- All three translators agree. The niphal *venissa* ("and it shall be carried") is passive, indicating the table is the object carried.

> **[fn29] v.29 -- *Ve'asita qe'arotav vekhappotav uqesotav umenaqiyyotav asher yussakh bahen zahav tahor ta'aseh otam*: "And you shall make its dishes and its ladles and its jars and its bowls with which it will be poured out; of pure gold you shall make them"** -- EMET renders "dishes...ladles...jars...bowls." LOGOS renders "plates...ladles...pitchers...bowls." PNEUMA renders "plates and dishes...pitchers and bowls." The consensus follows EMET for the four distinct vessels: "dishes" (*qe'arot*), "ladles" (*kappot*, palm-shaped scoops), "jars" (*qesot*, vessels for drink offerings), and "bowls" (*menaqiyyot*, pouring vessels). LOGOS's "plates" for *qe'arot* is acceptable, but "dishes" is closer to the Hebrew, which denotes shallow serving vessels. LOGOS's "pitchers" for *qesot* is less precise than "jars." PNEUMA conflates the four into two pairs, losing the distinct enumeration. The verb *yussakh* (hophal of *nasakh*, "to pour out") refers to the libation function of these vessels.

> **[fn30] v.30 -- *Venatattha al-hashulchan lechem panim lefanai tamid*: "And you shall set upon the table the bread of the Presence before me continually"** -- All three translators agree. *Lechem panim* (literally "bread of the face/presence") denotes the twelve loaves set before YHWH's face as a perpetual offering (Lev 24:5-9), renewed every Sabbath. *Tamid* ("continually, perpetually") indicates the bread is never absent from the table.

> **[fn31] v.31 -- *Ve'asita menorat zahav tahor miqshah te'aseh hammenorah yerekhah veqanah gevi'eha kaftoreha ufracheha mimmennah yihyu*: "And you shall make a lampstand of pure gold; of hammered work the lampstand shall be made. Its base and its shaft, its cups, its knobs, and its blossoms shall be from it -- of one piece with it"** -- EMET renders "its base and its shaft, its cups, its knobs, and its blossoms shall be from it -- of one piece with it." LOGOS renders "Its base and its shaft, its cups, its buds and its blossoms -- from it they shall be." PNEUMA renders "its base, its shaft, its cups, its buds, and its blossoms are all to be one piece with it." The consensus follows EMET, adding the clarifying dash phrase "of one piece with it" (which makes explicit what *mimmennah yihyu* implies). For *kaftorim*, see Key Decisions ("knobs" preferred over "buds"). For *yerekah*, all render "base" -- the word literally means "thigh" but denotes the pedestal of the lampstand.

> **[fn32] v.32 -- *Veshishah qanim yotse'im mitsiddeyha shloshah qenei menorah mitsiddah ha'echad ushloshah qenei menorah mitsiddah hassheni*: "And six branches going out from its sides: three branches of the lampstand from its one side and three branches of the lampstand from its second side"** -- All three translators agree. The six lateral branches plus the central shaft make seven in total.

> **[fn33] v.33 -- *Shloshah gevi'im meshukqadim baqqaneh ha'echad kaftor vaferach ushloshah gevi'im meshukqadim baqqaneh ha'echad kaftor vaferach ken lesheshet haqqanim hayyotse'im min-hammenorah*: "Three cups shaped like almond blossoms on the one branch -- a knob and a blossom; and three cups shaped like almond blossoms on the other branch -- a knob and a blossom; so for the six branches going out from the lampstand"** -- All three translators agree in substance. The *meshukqadim* (pual participle of *shaqad*, "almond; to watch") means "made in almond-blossom shape." Each branch has a set of cup-knob-blossom ornaments. The almond (*shaqed*) puns on "watchful" (cf. Jer 1:11-12).

> **[fn34] v.34 -- *Ubammenorah arba'ah gevi'im meshukqadim kaftoreha ufracheha*: "And on the lampstand four cups shaped like almond blossoms -- its knobs and its blossoms"** -- All three translators agree. The central shaft has four almond-blossom cups, one more than each lateral branch.

> **[fn35] v.35 -- *Vekaftor tachat shnei haqqanim mimmennah vekaftor tachat shnei haqqanim mimmennah vekaftor tachat shnei haqqanim mimmennah lesheshet haqqanim hayyotse'im min-hammenorah*: "And a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it, and a knob under the two branches from it -- for the six branches going out from the lampstand"** -- All three translators agree. Three knobs on the central shaft, each at the junction where a pair of branches extends, giving the lampstand an organic, tree-like structure.

> **[fn36] v.36 -- *Kaftoreihem uqenotam mimmennah yihyu kullahh miqshah achat zahav tahor*: "Their knobs and their branches shall be from it; all of it one hammered work of pure gold"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "one hammered work." LOGOS renders "one hammered piece." PNEUMA renders "a single piece of hammered pure gold." The consensus follows EMET -- "one hammered work" preserves *miqshah achat* as a technical construction term. The entire lampstand, with all its components, is beaten from a single piece of gold.

> **[fn37] v.37 -- *Ve'asita et-neroteyha shiv'ah vehe'elah et-neroteyha vehe'ir al-ever paneyha*: "And you shall make its lamps seven, and he shall set up its lamps and it shall give light upon the space before it"** -- EMET renders "and he shall set up its lamps and it shall give light upon the space in front of it." LOGOS renders "and he shall set up its lamps and give light upon the area before it." PNEUMA renders "set them up so that they give light to the space in front of it." The consensus follows EMET. The verb *he'elah* (hiphil of *alah*, "to go up") means "he shall cause to go up," i.e., "he shall set up/light" the lamps. The subject shifts from "you" (Moses) to "he" (the priest), anticipating the priestly service. *Ever paneyha* ("the space of its face") indicates the lampstand illuminates forward, toward the table of the bread of the Presence across the Holy Place.

> **[fn38] v.38 -- *Umalqacheyha umachtoteyha zahav tahor*: "And its tongs and its firepans of pure gold"** -- EMET renders "tongs...firepans." LOGOS renders "tongs...fire pans." PNEUMA renders "tongs...trays." The consensus follows EMET -- "firepans" for *makhtotot* (see Key Decisions). These are the accessories for lamp maintenance: tongs for trimming wicks and firepans for collecting ash and charred material.

> **[fn39] v.39 -- *Kikkar zahav tahor ya'aseh otah et kol-hakkelim ha'elleh*: "A talent of pure gold he shall make it, with all these vessels"** -- All three translators agree. A *kikkar* ("talent") is approximately 75 pounds (34 kg) of pure gold -- the entire lampstand and all its accessories are made from this single mass.

> **[fn40] v.40 -- *Ur'eh va'aseh betavnitam asher-attah mar'eh bahar*: "And see and make according to their pattern which you are being shown on the mountain"** -- All three translators agree in substance. EMET renders "And see and make according to their pattern which you are being shown on the mountain." LOGOS renders "And see and make them according to their pattern which you are being shown on the mountain." PNEUMA renders "See to it that you make them according to the pattern you were shown on the mountain." The consensus follows EMET. The paired imperatives *ur'eh* ("see!") and *va'aseh* ("make!") create a see-and-do sequence. The hophal participle *mar'eh* ("being shown, being caused to see") indicates Moses is the passive recipient of a divine visual revelation. PNEUMA's "you were shown" (past tense) changes the ongoing nature of the revelation implied by the participle. The recurrence of *tavnit* from v. 9 completes the chapter's inclusio, framing the entire section as instruction to replicate a heavenly archetype.

---

*Translated from the Leningrad Codex (B19A) with reference to Codex Vaticanus (LXX). All transliterations follow standard academic conventions. Hebrew text follows the Masoretic vocalization.*
